steps.title: Identify the grammatical relationship at the blank
steps.content: The sentence ends with "in fellow OECD member ______ nation Lithuania." The phrase "nation Lithuania" is a restrictive appositive: "Lithuania" specifies which OECD member nation is meant. A restrictive (essential) appositive must not be separated from the noun it identifies by any punctuation — adding a comma, semicolon, or dash would imply the name is supplementary information, which it is not.

steps.title: Eliminate the wrong choices and confirm D
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'nation, Lithuania.': A comma signals a non-restrictive appositive, implying "nation" already pinpoints a unique referent without the name. Here the name is essential to identify which nation, so the comma is wrong. ✗
  • Choice B — 'nation; Lithuania.': A semicolon must join two independent clauses; "in fellow OECD member nation" is not an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice C — 'nation—Lithuania.': A dash sets off parenthetical or supplementary information. "Lithuania" is essential here, not supplementary. ✗
  • Choice D — 'nation Lithuania.': No punctuation between "nation" and "Lithuania" — the name sits directly against the noun it identifies, correctly forming a restrictive appositive. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
